aliases: 蚵烙 (Hé Luò) / 蚵仔煎 (Kē Zǐ Jiān)

chinese: 蚵烙 (Hé Luò) / 蚵仔煎 (Kē Zǐ Jiān)

region_of_origin: Singapore (Teochew origins from Guangdong province, China)
food_subgroup: Hawker egg omelette with fresh oysters, tapioca starch batter, cooked in lard, served with chilli sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for oyster omelette as a distinct concept. Closest defensible parent: egg omelette with seafood (FOODON:03302757 egg dish). No exact match available; null is appropriate.
ontology.foodex2_description: Oyster — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for the primary seafood component. The composite dish (oyster omelette with starch batter) has no dedicated FoodEx2 code; A0CKP (oyster) is the best available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for the composite dish. A0CKP (oyster) used as primary ingredient anchor. Composite hawker dish not represented in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for oyster omelette as a prepared dish. Not clinically coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of oyster omelette weighs approximately 200g (oysters, egg, tapioca starch batter, lard, and bean sprouts, before chilli sauce), providing approximately 320 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster Omelette (Or Lua) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oyster Omelette (Or Lua), the primary approved regional source for Singapore hawker dishes. Values represent the composite dish as served at Singapore hawker centres.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — tapioca starch and analogous starch-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore hawker oyster omelette. GI of 72 is estimated from tapioca starch as the dominant carbohydrate component (GI ~70–85 per University of Sydney GI Database). The high fat and protein content from eggs and oysters moderates the effective GI slightly. GL calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 26g) / 100 = 18.72, rounded to 18.7 for a 200g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~250mg/100g; ~500mg per 200g plate before chilli sauce; total ~680mg with 30g chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or omit the accompanying chilli sauce, which adds approximately 180mg sodium, and be aware that a single plate contributes roughly 28–34% of the 2000mg daily sodium target recommended by HPB Singapore.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI tapioca starch batter (GI ~72); glycemic load ~18.7 per 200g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The tapioca starch batter drives a medium-high GI of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of ~18.7 per plate, which is moderate-high; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish occasionally and pair it with low-GI foods to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4g/100g (~8g per plate) from lard; cholesterol ~95mg/100g (~190mg per plate)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The traditional lard-cooked preparation delivers approximately 8g of saturated fat per plate, representing 40% of the 20g daily saturated fat limit; patients with cardiovascular disease should request vegetable oil versions where available, which reduce saturated fat by approximately 60%, and limit portion frequency to occasional consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional zinc source (~5.5mg/100g, ~11mg per plate meeting full daily RDA); high saturated fat from lard is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The oysters provide exceptional zinc (~11mg per 200g plate, meeting the full daily RDA of 8–11mg), which supports immune regulation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, but the high saturated fat from lard (~8g per plate) may promote systemic inflammation; vegetable oil versions are preferable for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Or Lua (Oyster Omelette, also known as Orh Luak or 蚝烙) is one of Singapore's most iconic Teochew-Hokkien hawker dishes, with origins in Guangdong province, China. The dish is prepared by frying fresh, plump oysters with a thick slurry of tapioca starch and beaten eggs on a very hot wok or flat griddle, traditionally using lard as the cooking fat. The result is a distinctive omelette with crispy, golden-brown edges and a gooey, chewy, starchy centre — a textural contrast that defines the dish. Bean sprouts are added for freshness and crunch. The dish is seasoned with fish sauce and white pepper and is always served with a tangy, spicy chilli sauce (often with lime or vinegar) that cuts through the richness of the fried egg and oysters. Nutritionally, the oysters are the standout ingredient: they are exceptionally rich in zinc (~5.5mg/100g composite, ~11mg per 200g plate — meeting the full adult daily RDA), vitamin B12 (3mcg/100g), and iron (1.5mg/100g). The lard-cooked preparation adds significant saturated fat (~4g/100g, ~8g per plate) and cholesterol (~95mg/100g). The tapioca starch batter drives a medium-high glycemic index of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of ~18.7 per 200g serving. Sodium is moderate at ~250mg/100g before sauce (~500mg per plate), rising to ~680mg with chilli sauce. The dish is not halal in its traditional form due to lard. It is gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it warrants moderation for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (high GL), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from lard), and hypertension (sodium accumulation with sauce). It is a zinc-rich hawker option with genuine micronutrient value from the oysters.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singapore hawker dishes. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate from tapioca starch (University of Sydney GI Database) with documented basis, which is the best available evidence for this composite dish.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per HPB source for this dish; lard does not contribute measurable industrial trans fat. Vitamin K is 0.0 in the source input — confirmed trace per source; oysters and eggs contribute minimal vitamin K (not separately reported in per_100g template fields). GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca starch as the dominant carbohydrate; no direct GI measurement exists for Singapore hawker oyster omelette. The 200g serving is used as the reference portion (HPB Singapore standard). Portions of 280g and 350g are also documented from other sources. The dish is not halal in traditional preparation (lard). Vegetable oil versions reduce saturated fat by approximately 60%. Chilli sauce (~30g) adds approximately 180mg additional sodium not reflected in per_100g values. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not reported by the source, hence -1.0.
